1.synthesis: The father warns that the lips of the forbidden woman “drip honey,” but “in the end she is bitter as wormwood… her feet go down to death” (vv.3–5). He urges, “Keep your way far from her” (v.8), lest one give his honor to others and at the end groan, “How I hated discipline, and my heart despised reproof!” (v.12). The integrative thread is the deadly deception of sexual sin: wisdom unmasks the sweet allure of adultery as a path that ends in bitterness, ruin, and bitter regret — warning the young to keep far from temptation’s door before the seduction takes hold, for the pleasure is fleeting and the destruction lasting.
1.historical_context: The “forbidden woman” (the adulteress) (v.3) is a recurring figure in Proverbs 1–9, sometimes also personifying Folly over against Lady Wisdom. The honey-then-wormwood contrast (vv.3–4) exposes the deceptive sweetness of illicit desire. The counsel to “keep your way far from her” (v.8) is the strategy of avoidance, and the closing lament (vv.12–14) voices the regret of one who spurned correction.
1.literary_structure: This discourse (vv.1–14) develops the adultery warning through the honey/wormwood and the path-to-death imagery, climaxing in the imagined regret of the ruined (vv.11–14). It sets up the contrasting call to marital faithfulness in the chapter’s second half.
1.theological_application: Sexual sin is deceptively sweet but deadly in its end (vv.3–5; James 1:15, “sin… brings forth death”), and wisdom’s strategy is distance: “keep your way far from her” (v.8; 2 Timothy 2:22, “flee youthful passions”). The bitter regret of the ruined (v.12) warns that despising reproof leads to lasting sorrow. The believer learns to guard against temptation by avoidance and to heed correction now, before the allure ensnares. Sexual sin is deceptively sweet but deadly in its end (vv.3–5; James 1:15), and wisdom’s strategy is distance: “keep your way far from her” (v.8; 2 Timothy 2:22). The bitter regret of the ruined (v.12) warns that despising reproof leads to lasting sorrow. The believer learns to guard against temptation by avoidance rather than flirtation, and to heed correction now, before the allure takes hold and the path bends down toward death.
1.christology: The warning against the seductress and the call to faithfulness reflect the deeper biblical drama of covenant fidelity: idolatry is spiritual adultery (James 4:4), and Christ is the faithful Bridegroom who cleanses and keeps a pure bride for himself (Ephesians 5:25–27). The path of illicit sweetness ending in death (v.5) is the way from which Christ delivers, granting his people the new heart and the Spirit to walk in purity (1 Thessalonians 4:3–8).

15.synthesis: The father commends the joy of faithful marriage: “Drink water from your own cistern… Let your fountain be blessed, and rejoice in the wife of your youth… be intoxicated always in her love” (vv.15–19). He warns that a man’s ways are before the eyes of the LORD, and the wicked “is held fast in the cords of his sin” (vv.21–22). The integrative thread is the positive delight of covenant faithfulness over against adultery: wisdom does not merely forbid illicit sex but commends the exclusive, joyful intimacy of marriage as God’s good gift — the satisfying alternative to the bitter path warned against, lived out under the all-seeing eyes of the LORD.
15.historical_context: The imagery of one’s “own cistern” and “fountain” (v.15, v.18) celebrates exclusive marital intimacy, and “the wife of your youth” (v.18) commends lifelong covenant faithfulness (Malachi 2:14–15). The reminder that “a man’s ways are before the eyes of the LORD” (v.21) grounds sexual ethics in God’s omniscience, and the “cords of his sin” (v.22) depict sin’s enslaving power.
15.literary_structure: The second half of chapter 5 (vv.15–23) balances the warning of the first with the positive celebration of marital joy (vv.18–19), then closes with the sober reminder of divine scrutiny and sin’s entrapment (vv.21–23). The contrast of blessed fountain and binding cords frames the choice.
15.theological_application: Wisdom commends the joyful, exclusive intimacy of marriage as God’s good gift, the satisfying alternative to illicit desire — “rejoice in the wife of your youth” (v.18; Hebrews 13:4). All our ways are open before the LORD (v.21), and unrepented sin enslaves (v.22; John 8:34). The believer is taught to find delight in faithful marriage, to live coram Deo (before God’s face), and to flee the bondage that sin weaves around those who indulge it. Wisdom commends the joyful, exclusive intimacy of marriage as God’s good gift, the satisfying alternative to illicit desire — “rejoice in the wife of your youth” (v.18; Hebrews 13:4). All our ways are open before the LORD (v.21), and unrepented sin enslaves with its own cords (v.22; John 8:34). The believer is taught to delight in faithful marriage, to live before God’s all-seeing face, and to flee the bondage that sin weaves around those who indulge it.
15.christology: The blessing of faithful marriage points to the union of Christ and his church, the great mystery marriage signifies (Ephesians 5:31–32). The man held fast “in the cords of his sin” (v.22) is the one Christ came to free, for whom the Son sets at liberty truly frees (John 8:36). Living before the all-seeing eyes of the LORD (v.21) is, for the believer, life before the One who knows all yet, in Christ, covers our sin and grants a clean heart (Hebrews 4:13; 1 John 1:7).
